variables.content: Providing an agent with access to a curated set of MCP tools—defined as callable programmatic interfaces that perform application-level or OS-level operations—and making those tools selectable at each action step causally increases the agent’s task success rate (accuracy) and typically reduces Average Completion Steps (ACS) for most evaluated large multimodal models under the stated experimental conditions, when other experimental factors (agent configuration, model version, and step limits) are held constant, so that the deliberate change between conditions is the presence versus absence of MCP tools.

A capability-path evaluation corpus composed of 163 LangChain tools, 107 CrewAI tools, 79 MCP tools, 12 AutoGen tools, and 8 smolagents tools (total 369 tools) provides a representative sample of real-world tool naming and capability-organization patterns such that deterministic mapping outcomes on this corpus are informative about the broader production agent ecosystem's naming behavior.

Because the QLCoder toolbox is exposed via MCP servers, the system can be ported to other LLM-based coding agents by reconfiguring the agent command-line interface to invoke a different agent binary; in experiments that substituted the primary Claude Sonnet 4-based agent with Gemini CLI (Gemini 2.5 Flash) and Codex (GPT-5 minimal) while keeping the same MCP tooling, integration via MCP increased the alternative agents' compilation rates (for example, Gemini's compilation rate increased from 19% without tools to 24% with tools, and Codex's compilation rate increased from 0% without tools to 24% with tools), but these alternative agents did not produce any fully successful synthesized queries in our 20-CVE experiments, indicating that MCP tooling improves syntactic validity while final synthesis success depends substantially on the base agent's reasoning and code-generation capability.

Tool sets $T_i$ are attack surfaces in tool-integrated agent systems: compromised tools can return manipulated outputs that affect agent behavior, and in multi-agent settings malicious tools used by one agent can indirectly impact other agents through collaboration patterns such as planner-executor workflows. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) introduces additional supply-chain risks because poisoned MCP tools can embed malicious instructions in tool descriptions, and MCP mechanisms that dynamically request additional information from client agents (e.g., content sampling mechanisms) create further attack surfaces including data leakage and manipulation.

MCPAgent is a baseline agent implementation, built by adapting the OpenHands CodeAct agent, that treats tool discovery as an explicit MCP tool: the environment provides a gateway MCP server implementing find_tools(query,num_tools) and call_tool(tool_name,args); each MCP tool's JSON specification (the UTF-8 JSON serialization containing keys name (string), description (string), and inputSchema (a JSON object listing argument names, JSON types, and per-argument descriptions)) is embedded into a dense vector index, the find_tools function embeds the LLM-generated natural-language query and returns the top-k tool specifications ranked by cosine similarity between query and tool vectors, and call_tool accepts a chosen tool name and argument values and invokes the corresponding underlying MCP tool; in the reported experiments the find_tools function used dense embeddings of the tool JSON specification (the main runs used OpenAI text-embedding-3-large), returned five tools by default, allowed the LLM to iteratively generate new queries and infer dependencies during execution, and MCPAgent retained CodeAct's auxiliary tools (Think, Python, Shell, Web fetch, File edit) while replacing direct browser access with the gateway find_tools/call_tool architecture.

Define compilation rate as the fraction of candidate CodeQL queries proposed by an agent that successfully compile under the target CodeQL language-server and validator; the proposition is that integrating the MCP tooling (CodeQL LSP and retrieval-augmented database) with an alternative agent causally increased that agent's compilation-rate metric—for example, Gemini CLI's compilation rate rose from 19% without tools to 24% with tools, and Codex's compilation rate rose from 0% without tools to 24% with tools—because the agent could use syntax-aware completions and documentation lookups to avoid syntactic and API-name errors, assuming the agent actually invoked MCP calls as intended and the reported compilation-rate changes are not confounded by unrelated run-to-run variance.

PentestMCP provides concrete primitives — MCP server processes that export documented tool calls describing inputs, function, and outputs — that allow a developer to construct and customize multi‑agent penetration‑testing workflows: an agent implementation can include different MCP servers at run‑time, select which exported tools to call, plan ordered sequences of tool invocations that implement cyber kill‑chain steps, and substitute or update server implementations or individual tools without changing the agent's core control code, enabling flexible composition of multi‑function agents that can be updated with new prompts, tools, and knowledge sources.

ToolUniverse is implemented as an extensible software ecosystem that can connect to and wrap any open‑ or closed‑weight large language model (LLM), large reasoning model (LRM), or AI agent without requiring additional model fine‑tuning; once connected to a model, the environment enables that model to act as a customizable AI scientist that can plan, find tools using the Find Tool operation, and call tools using the Call Tool operation. The ecosystem maintains a continuously updated registry of callable tools that, as reported in the project materials, exceeds 600 entries and covers categories including machine‑learning model endpoints, agentic tools, scientific software packages, automation and workflow primitives, visualization utilities, safety and human‑feedback tools, and local and remote dataset access tools; the project reports category counts (as of October 11, 2025) of 17 ML‑model tools, 56 agent tools, 164 scientific‑package tools, 8 automation tools, 11 workflow tools, 3 visualization tools, 6 feedback/safety tools, and 391 local and remote dataset tools.

The collected real-world corpus is defined as the set of Python-based tools obtained by crawling three named public tool platforms (mcp.so, mcpservers, and mcpmarket), resolving and retaining publicly accessible GitHub repositories with identifiable entry points, and filtering for accessibility and English descriptions to yield $N=10{,}573$ tools. The weak assumption is that this corpus constitutes a representative sample of the deployed LLM-agent tool ecosystem for the purposes of evaluating detection performance and embedding realism—i.e., that the observed category distributions and code-size statistics (for example, 88% of tools under 100 lines of code) and the functional diversity in the collected set reflect typical production tooling rather than being heavily biased by platform-specific selection, crawler heuristics, or filtering decisions.

Combining the empirical GitHub measurements that show validated public MCP servers are typically small, single-maintainer projects dominated by repetitive scaffolding (median SLOC = 920, median contributors = 1, median churn = 31 LoC/month) with the AutoMCP evaluation result that the compiler produced runnable MCP servers for roughly 77% of sampled tool calls out-of-the-box and for 99.9% of sampled calls after applying small, localized OpenAPI fixes (median ≈19 lines changed per fixed spec) implies that the dominant impediment to broad MCP adoption is the quality and completeness of OpenAPI specifications (for example, missing or incorrect security metadata, unresolved base-URL placeholders, undocumented runtime headers, and occasional parameter-type mismatches) rather than the absence of reliable code generation technology; therefore, investing in improved OpenAPI authoring practices, linting, validation, and automated specification-repair tooling constitutes a high-leverage intervention to scale agent-ready tool ecosystems, while acknowledging that non-spec barriers such as organizational, security, or operational constraints may also affect adoption.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is defined here as a protocol that specifies a structured context exchange format in which an AI agent calls a callable tool and the tool returns a structured data object (for example, a JSON object containing named telemetry fields such as timestamped counters, per-port packet-drop rates, and formatted device metadata). The proposition asserted as a design premise is: exposing modular, task-specific tools to an AI agent via an MCP-formatted tool call interface—where a "tool" is a callable software adapter that returns structured telemetry or performs an action, and where the platform routes agent calls to these adapters and returns their structured outputs—reduces the need for the agent to parse raw heterogeneous telemetry logs and thereby materially lowers the agent's domain-specific parsing burden and enables more robust, composable diagnostic reasoning pipelines for root-cause analysis. This premise assumes (i) that tools can produce sufficiently expressive structured outputs to support troubleshooting tasks and (ii) that agents can consume MCP-structured outputs without requiring bespoke parsing logic per telemetry source.
      [14] Model Context Protocol Project. 2025. Model Context Protocol: Introduction. https://modelcontextprotocol.io/introduction. Accessed: 2025-05-31.

When MCP services generated by Code2MCP were added to existing consumer-side agent systems in controlled integration experiments, the augmented configurations outperformed their non-augmented counterparts: integrating Code2MCP-generated tools into a RepoMaster-based workflow that otherwise operates at source-code level increased task success rates and reduced average interaction steps for the fixed task set used in the experiments, and augmenting an OpenAgents planning system with Code2MCP-supplied MCP tools increased both task success rate and task coverage across the evaluated general and scientific task groups. Under identical task specifications, success conditions, and interaction/runtime budgets, these improvements indicate that automatically produced MCP services can be discovered and exploited by standard selection and planning strategies to improve end-to-end agent task performance.

In the latter half of calendar year 2025, frontier reasoning model instances exemplified by the vendor labels "GPT‑5.1‑codex‑max" (OpenAI) and "Opus 4.5" (Anthropic) exhibit substantially increased usable context sizes and improved context management, reductions in hallucination rates, and improved instruction‑following fidelity compared to prior mainstream models; concurrently, tooling improvements such as adoption of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for structured agent communication and context orchestration and integrated development environment (IDE) / tool integrations that provide separate planning and review tools and multi‑agent support (example: Cursor’s planning and review features) have become available. These combined model and tooling improvements materially mitigate previously limiting failure modes in AI‑assisted development—specifically limited long‑range context, uncontrolled hallucination, and poor agent coordination—and, when these models and tools are used within an explicitly staged workflow that enforces decomposition, canonical planning, automated testing, and human oversight, they make multi‑agent workflows practicable for substantial, multi‑file, large‑codebase software development.

MCPWorld includes an MCPClient that dynamically connects to configured MCP servers, automatically discovers available MCP tools including names, descriptions, and input schemas, represents discovered MCP tools in the same tool format used by local tools as `anthropic.types.beta.BetaToolParam`, transparently invokes MCP tools on behalf of the agent, and returns consistent ToolResult objects to the agent. The MCPClient also manages MCP session lifecycle and resource cleanup.

A reproducible large-scale dataset was collected between late June and early July 2025 that comprises 67,057 MCP servers harvested from six public registries—four decentralized registries (mcp.so, MCP Market, MCP Store, Pulse MCP) and two centralized registries (Smithery and npm)—and, from the Python-based servers among the decentralized-registry corpus, static analysis of repository contents using Python Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) parsing to identify function definitions annotated with an @mcp.tool()–style decorator extracted 44,499 distinct tools (tool names, textual descriptions when present, and observed return-value patterns) for quantitative analysis.
